reviews.author: Dennis Kaiser
reviews.comment:
This is by far the worst agent experience I've had with any of the tools: 
 
 Every time I start a new chat, multiple old and unrelated files are opened in the editor 
 I don't see any option to completely disable the automatic "add file to context" feature. I have to turn them off manually every time. I do not want to automatically add files to the context. This has led to unrelated files being added to the context numerous times which is a huge waste of tokens and context space. Just give me an option to disable this feature entirely, especially in agent mode. 
 The agent is still unable to read the terminal output most of the time 
 For about a week the model's output is missing characters, making it almost impossible to properly read what the model is telling me 
 I do not see any option to show me how full the current context is. This is super relevant to effectively work with the agent 
 I did not manage to configure skills in a way where the agent picks them up reliably 
 The feature to accept/decline edits made by the agent triggers so many edge cases it's hilarious. Make the agent change a file and then make the agent move it using a terminal command? The Changes are gone. Numerous bugs with the accept/decline buttons not rendering properly or rendering on top of completely unrelated UI elements (even on top of other/unrelated applications) 
 Configuring auto accepts for certain terminal calls is extremely cumbersome and hard to debug. I have not found any documentation for that and was forced to enable intellij logs to find out what the problem was. 
 When asked to accept a terminal command, there is no easy way to say "allow all uses of...". I have to open the settings (which is a modal, btw. I can't change the settings and use the agent at the same time) and manually add some regex to the table of allowed terminal commands. This is super cumbersome.

reviews.author: rupert.jung
reviews.comment: Works better than in the past. But I still have to press "Continue" all the time just to edit files. Also it always to to run tests with maven instead of using the IntelliJ runner for this which slows down things dramatically. Also, the IDE becomes very slow when the agent is active.

reviews.author: Enes Oscar
reviews.comment:
Overall, it provides a great experience. I get especially impressive results with the Sonnet and Opus models. Recently, I also integrated my database via MCP, which helped complete my development ecosystem. Kudos to the IntelliJ developers for their great work. 
 There are occasional rate-limit issues, but I use GitHub Copilot across multiple editors and see the same limitation elsewhere as well. I’m confident this will be resolved in the near future. I will continue using GitHub Copilot with IntelliJ.

reviews.author: rockhw_huang
reviews.comment: GitHub Copilot's sign-in feature isn't working properly on Windows 11 ARM, and the chat page just keeps spinning/loading forever. I've tried it on both IntelliJ IDEA 2023 and IDEA 2025 (ARM versions), and both have the same issues.

reviews.author: Tamas Bela
reviews.comment:
It works, but it's not usable and may be a good enough reason at this point for switching from PyCharm to VS Code altogether. It's sad that this doesn't get more attention to get it on par with what VS code can do, or at least close to it.
Why is auto-approve not working? it's annoying to get prompted for every little thing to continue. The text editor is broken: it doesn't handle multi lines properly, it acts like one single line. Pressing end will not go to the end of line, but instead at the end of the whole text. When adding new lines at the end, it takes a fragment from the last part and there's no way to really navigate to the end. It's riddled with bugs overall.

reviews.comment: Since installing the latest version (1.5.66-243), the entire IDE (Rider) crashes almost every time. Even detached CoPilot windows can only be moved with considerable effort and patience. The functionality is currently virtually non-existent. An update is urgently needed.

reviews.author: Per Holm-Petersen
reviews.comment: I see a lot of negative reviews, so I thought I'd give positive feedback. I use this A LOT. While the VS Code implementation is the best, the JetBrains implementation isn't half bad. I'd wish that updates came out faster. You can wait weeks or months waiting for an update that's already out in VS Code. I'd also love to see more of the deliberations, which as shown in VS Code. And the Rider implementation keeps inserting byte order marks near the top of my files that have to be manually discarded. I've also seen more misfired in Rider, where it for example deletes most of a file and then complains that it's missing. It's OK, and can just cancel it, but that never happens in VS Code. The personality also seems different. Claude Opus 4.5 is more of a clear thinker in VS Code than it is in Rider, where I get more ready-fire-aim. You should tell the model that it's OK to be unsure or need more info. Being overconfident to the point of gaslighting is ChatGPT's job.

reviews.author: Pawel Milosz
reviews.comment:
GitHub Copilot for PhpStorm – Great Idea, Rough Execution  
 ★★☆☆☆ 
 I really wanted to love this plugin. The concept is solid, and when it works, it genuinely helps with day-to-day development. But after a few weeks of daily use in PhpStorm, the list of frustrations has grown long enough that I feel obligated to warn others. 
  Terminal blindness 
The most baffling issue: Copilot Chat happily runs commands in the terminal — php -l, cd, you name it — but then acts as if nothing happened. It executes the command and completely ignores the output, leaving you to manually copy-paste results back into the chat just to continue the conversation. For an AI assistant that's supposed to streamline your workflow, this is a pretty fundamental failure. Agent mode helps slightly, but it's inconsistent. 
  Text selection goes haywire 
Selecting code in the editor while the Copilot panel is active is an exercise in frustration. The selection frequently jumps, shifts, or ends up somewhere entirely unintended. Simple actions like highlighting a method or a block of code become unreliable, which in a tool you use dozens of times per hour is genuinely disruptive. 
  The cursor teleports 
Related to the above — the plugin has a habit of moving the editor cursor to seemingly random locations on its own. You're working in one file, Copilot does something in the background, and suddenly your view or cursor position has jumped somewhere else entirely. It breaks focus and flow constantly. 
  System freezes 
This one is the dealbreaker. Periodically — and unpredictably — the plugin freezes the entire IDE and sometimes the whole system for 20 to 40 seconds. No warning, no spinner, just a complete lockup. On a machine that handles Docker, Kubernetes and multiple services without breaking a sweat, this is hard to excuse. It appears to happen most often when the chat is processing or when switching between files rapidly. 
  Bottom line 
The underlying AI capabilities are promising, and I can see where this is headed. But right now the plugin feels like it shipped too early. The terminal integration is broken by design, the editor interactions are glitchy, and the freezes make it actively harmful to productivity at times. I'll revisit when these basics are fixed — until then, I can't recommend it for serious daily use.

reviews.author: David Brouse
reviews.comment:
Overall, the plugin is really good.  They just have a quality control issue with their releases. 
 In DataGrip 2025.3.5, I had auto-update turned on, and when the newest version of the Copilot plugin 1.5.65-243  (from 2026/02/12) got installed, the plugin crashed on startup.  I turned off auto-update, and downgraded to version 1.5.63-243 (from 2020/01/15) using the "Install from disk" process, and everything is working again.

reviews.author: Aleksei Krasnov
reviews.comment:
I am fed up with the constant technical glitches / pitfalls in Copilot; it’s making my work impossible.
I have been using Copilot since mid 2025, and it worked perfectly.
After new releases in 2026, it becomes impossible to use.  Basically, it freezes DataSpell completely when I'm trying to open Copilot/or write something there, and the only solution is to kill DataSpell.
Now I switched to the previous release of Copilot 1.5.62-243 together with DataSpell 2025.2.3, and it works more or less better, still running intensively CPU when I'm trying to write in Copilot chat.
